zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Richard Lacon於1379年出生在英國英格蘭武斯特斯爾馬德雷斯費爾德時，他的父親William Lygon31歲，母親Margaret Paslewe12歲。他與Alice Walcot在英國史洛普爾結婚。他們至少是1個兒子的父母。他去世於1446年10月8日在他的家鄉享年67歲。

nameMeanings.name: Lacon
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: 1  English: locative name from Lacon in Wem (Shrops). This name has probably been confused with or absorbed by   Lakin   (1), especially in counties adjoining Shrops, such as Staffs.

2 English: while the name in Yorks could be that in (1) through migration, its appearance in late 14th-century Clint in the north-west of WR Yorks, and the persistent final -can in most early forms of the surname, suggest that it may have had a different but unknown origin.

variants.name: Lakin
meanings: Americanized form of Jewish Leykin (from Belarus), a metronymic from Leyke, a pet form of the Yiddish female personal name Leye, from the Hebrew female personal name Lea, from which English Leah is derived (see Genesis 29: 16) + the Slavic possessive suffix -in.
English: patronymic from the Middle English personal name Lawkin, a diminutive of Law- (a pet form of the personal name Laurence; see Lawrence ). Compare Law 1 and Larkin .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Richard Lacon was born in 1379, in Madresfield, Worcestershire, England, United Kingdom, his father, William Lygon, was 31 and his mother, Margaret Paslewe, was 12. He married Alice Walcot in Shropshire, England. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died on 8 October 1446, in his hometown, at the age of 67.

Il a épousé Alice Walcot à Shropshire, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé le 8 octobre 1446, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 67 ans.

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Richard Lacon(이)가 1379년 영국 잉글랜드 워스스터실르 매드레스필드에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 William Lygon(은)는 31세였고, 그의 어머니 Margaret Paslewe(은)는 12세였다. 그는 영국 슈럽실르에서 Alice Walcot(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1446년 10월 8일 그의 고향에서 67세의 나이로 사망했다.
